title: Relationships Between Socioeconomic and Demographic Variables and Drug Overdose Death Rates
text:
Over half of Americans report knowing someone personally who has died of an overdose (American Addiction Centers, 2024). Drug overdose deaths remain a major public health crisis in the United States, affecting individuals across all socioeconomic, demographic, and geographic backgrounds. Overdose mortality rates have risen steadily since at least 1999, with a dramatic upsurge seen in the past decade (see Figure 1). While this issue is often discussed in broad terms, frequently framed as a uniform epidemic affecting the population as a whole (e.g., the “opioid epidemic”), the underlying drivers of overdose risk are complex and multifaceted. In particular, socioeconomic factors such as income, poverty, and educational attainment may play an important role in shaping the prevalence and distribution of overdose deaths across the country. Understanding these relationships is critical for both informing policy and targeting interventions to protect vulnerable groups.
(National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2024)
This study aims to answer the following questions:

How are median household income, poverty rate, and educational attainment related to drug overdose death rates in states, and do these relationships vary across different drugs?
Does the effect of poverty on overdose death rates depend on educational attainment of the population?
Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between males and females?
Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between different age groups?

title: Conclusion
text:
An important finding of this study is that no single socioeconomic variable consistently explains overdose death rates across all drug types. Instead, each category of drug appears to have its own profile of risk factors for overdose mortality, influenced by an interaction of factors beyond just those included in this study. Thus, studies of and prevention strategies for drug overdose deaths likely need to be tailored to specific substances rather than relying on a single overall approach. This highlights the need for continued research into the predictors of drug overdose rates, be they social, economic, healthcare, behavioral, or other.
In addition, the analysis demonstrates that overdose mortality rates vary significantly across different demographic groups. Our findings indicate that prevention and intervention strategies should consider gender-specific approaches as well as prioritize middle-aged adults.

text:
For each drug category, we choose the ridge regression model containing either hs_grad or bachelors based on the results above and add an interaction term between poverty and the education predictor, then compare the RMSE, MAE, and adjusted \(R^2\) of this model to those of the original model without the interaction term.
Ridge regression is used for this analysis because it is more robust to the collinearities among socioeconomic predictors. Using ridge regression allows the predictor variables to remain as consistent as possible across the different drug models since we can keep all socioeconomic variables in every model regardless of multicollinearities. Although linear regression models with variable selection achieved slightly better predictive performances for some drug categories, the ridge regression models performed similarly enough that the modest reduction in predictive accuracy was considered an acceptable tradeoff for improved consistency and comparability across models.

For drugs overall, heroin, and methamphetamine, the models without an interaction term between poverty rate and educational attainment levels performed slightly better. Conversely, the models with interaction terms performed slightly better for cocaine, prescription opioids, and benzodiazepines. These improvements were modest, however, suggesting that the effect of poverty on overdose mortality rates is relatively consistent across different levels of educational attainment. In other words, while both poverty and education are independently related to overdose mortality, the relationship between poverty and overdose death rates does not appear to substantially strengthen or weaken depending on the education level of the population.
At the same time, the similar patterns observed among the cocaine, prescription opioids, and benzodiazepines models mirror the similarities identified in the analysis for Research Question 1. These recurring patterns support the likelihood that additional contributing factors beyond the socioeconomic variables included in this study play an important role in overdose mortality for these substances.

title: Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between males and females?
text: We perform a Welch two sample t-test to compare overall overdose death rates between males and females. We set the significance level at 0.05.

title: Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between males and females?
text: We perform a Welch two sample t-test to compare overall overdose death rates between males and females. We set the significance level at 0.05.

title: Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between males and females?
text:
Results
The mean overdose death rate for males is approximately 46.26, and the mean overdose death rate for females is approximately 19.49. The t-test indicates a statistically significant difference in means (\(p<2.2e-16\)) with a 95% confidence interval of (23.03, 30.51).

title: Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between males and females?
text:
Analysis of Results
Males had significantly higher average overdose death rates than females, with the mean male overdose rate over twice the female rate. Possible explanations for this disparity are that males may be more likely to engage in high-risk substance-use behaviors, use larger quantities of drugs, or be less likely to seek treatment for substance-use disorders or mental health conditions. Zero is well outside the confidence interval for the difference in means, which further suggests that sex is an important demographic factor associated with overdose mortality, as it points to a practically meaningful, not just statistically significant, difference between sexes.

The 15-24 and 65+ age groups had the lowest overdose death rates and were not found to differ significantly from each other. In contrast, overdose death rates were significantly higher among adults age 25–64 compared to both groups. Mortality rates were relatively low in the 15-24 age group, then increased substantially, peaking among middle-aged groups, particularly individuals age 35-54, before dropping off sharply again in the 65+ age group. The 35-44 age group differed significantly from every age group except 45-54 and has the highest rates overall.
Several factors may contribute to this pattern of overdose mortality being concentrated among prime working-age adults. Individuals in middle adulthood may experience greater exposure to chronic stress, mental health challenges, or long-term substance dependence. The lower overdose death rates observed among younger individuals (ages 15–24) may reflect lower cumulative exposure to addictive substances or shorter durations of substance use. Similarly, the decline in overdose death rates among older individuals (ages 65 and older) may be a result of lower rates of illicit drug use or survivor effects. Differences in healthcare utilization or practices between age groups may contribute to differences in overdose death rates as well.

title: Research Question 1
text:
For each drug (including “all drugs”), we begin by creating two linear regression models containing med_income, poverty, and either hs_grad, or bachelors as predictors of the overdose death rate. We perform backward elimination on the models using 10-fold cross-validation
med_income was found to have a Variable Inflation Factor (VIF) greater than 5 in most of the models, indicating the (unsurprising) presence of (multi)collinearities between the socioeconomic variables. Thus, ridge regressions with 10-fold cross-validation are also performed using both initial sets of predictors, and the four models are compared by root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and adjusted \(R^2\). Baseline values for each of these statistics are included as well using a model that predicts the same overdose death rate for every observation. The baseline RMSE was computed using the mean as the predicted value, and the baseline MAE was computed using the median as the predicted value.
We choose the model with the highest adjusted \(R^2\) and lowest RMSE and MAE for comparisons across drug categories. In cases of conflicting differences (i.e., lower adjusted \(R^2\)/higher RMSE but lower MAE), we choose the model with the lowest MAE due to the skewed nature of the overdose death rate distributions. Residual plots for the chosen model for each drug are included on the “Residual Plots” page.
We set the significance level at 0.05 for all tests.

Poverty rate was the most consistent predictor across all models, with higher poverty levels pointing to higher overdose death rates for all drugs except cocaine, with which it was not found to be significantly associated. The proportion of the population with a bachelor’s degree or higher was found to be a significant predictor of overdose death rates for all drugs except heroin and benzodiazepines, but the direction of the relationship was not consistent across models. Although higher proportions of the population with a bachelor’s degree were associated with lower rates of methamphetamine overdose deaths, they were associated with higher rates of death for prescription opioids, cocaine, benzodiazepines, and overall drugs. One possible explanation for this finding is that increased urbanization may contribute to both a greater prevalence and availability of certain drugs as well as to larger populations of college-educated professionals. In contrast, heroin overdose death rates were better explained by the proportion of the population with a high school degree or higher. Median household income also followed inconsistent patterns across the models, with higher median income associated with higher overdose death rates for heroin and methamphetamine but lower death rates for cocaine and all drugs combined.
Overall, the results indicate that socioeconomic conditions are significant predictors of overdose mortality rates, although the strength and nature of the relationships vary considerably between drug categories. The adjusted \(R^2\) varied widely across models, from 0.60 in the cocaine model to 0.06 in the benzodiazepines model. The selected socioeconomic variables account for only about a third or less of the variance in the overdose death rate associated with every drug except cocaine, and they explain the least amount of variance in the overdose death rates of prescription opioids and benzodiazepines, the two drug groups in this study that are able to be obtained legally. This suggests that the factors, socioeconomic or otherwise, associated with overdose death rates may differ substantially across different drugs, and vulnerabilities to one substance may not transfer to another. Additionally, the similarities in performance between the ridge regression models and the models which underwent variable selection further highlight the complex relationship between overdose mortality and highly interconnected social and economic conditions.

title: Data
text:
Over the years 2020-2024, the CDC’s State Unintentional Drug Overdose Reporting System (SUDORS) compiled data on unintentional and undetermined-intent drug overdose deaths, stratified by drug type, age, and sex, in 33-43 states including DC. A portion of this Fatal Drug Overdose Data (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2026) was combined with income and educational attainment data from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED) (Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, 2026c, 2026b, 2026a) and poverty data from the US Census Bureau (U.S. Census Bureau, 2023, 2024, 2025). There are 187 observations.
We explore how the following variables can be used to explain and predict overdose death rates, given as an age-adjusted rate per 100,000 persons, for any/all drugs, heroin, prescription opioids, cocaine, methamphetamine, and benzodiazepines.
median_income
median household income in thousands (USD)


poverty
percentage of the population below the national poverty threshold (i.e., poverty rate)


hs_grad
percentage of the population 18 years and over with a high school diploma or higher


bachelors
percentage of the population 18 years and over with a bachelor’s degree or higher
The following graphs show the distribution of overdose death rates for each drug. We note that all of the distributions are unimodal and right-skewed.
We also present scatterplots illustrating relationships between each predictor variable and the overdose death rate for each drug type.

title: Data
text:
All drugs:
No clear relationship can be discerned between states’ drug overdose death rates and median household income, proportion of the population with a high school diploma or higher, or proportion of the population with a bachelor’s degree or higher. There appears to be a weak positive correlation between drug overdose deaths and poverty rate (\(r = 0.33\)).

title: Data
text:
Heroin:
No clear relationship can be discerned between states’ heroin overdose death rates and median household income, poverty rate, proportion of the population with a high school diploma or higher, or proportion of the population with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

title: Data
text:
Prescription Opioids:
Omitting a few outliers, no clear relationship can be discerned between states’ prescription opioid overdose death rates and median household income, poverty rate, proportion of the population with a high school diploma or higher, or proportion of the population with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

title: Data
text:
Cocaine:
No clear relationship can be discerned between states’ cocaine overdose death rates and median household income, poverty rate, proportion of the population with a high school diploma or higher. There appears to be a moderate positive correlation between cocaine overdose deaths and proportion of the population with a bachelors degree or higher (\(r = 0.67\)).

title: Data
text:
Methamphetamine:
There appears to be a moderate-to-weak negative correlation between states’ methamphetamine overdose death rates and median household income (\(r = -0.45\)) and a moderate-to-weak positive correlation between death rates and poverty rate (\(r = 0.48\)). Omitting a few outliers, no clear relationship can be discerned between methamphetamine overdose deaths and proportion of the population with a high school diploma or higher, but a moderate-to-strong negative relationship appears to exist between the death rate and proportion of the population holding a bachelors degree or higher.

title: Data
text:
Benzodiazepines:
Omitting a few outliers, no clear relationship can be discerned between states’ drug overdose death rates and median household income, poverty rate, proportion of the population with a high school diploma or higher, or proportion of the population with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

title: Limitations
text:
This study relied on state-level aggregate data, limiting the scope of the conclusions we can draw. Socioeconomic conditions within states are far from homogeneous, varying widely from city to city, and even neighborhood to neighborhood, and we cannot assume that relationships that exist at the state level can be generalized to its sublevels. For example, a positive relationship between median household income and overdose death rates at the state level does not imply that individuals with higher incomes are at greater risk of overdose deaths.
Another limitation was the availability and completeness of the data, or lack thereof. The age group consisting of children age 15 and younger were excluded from the analysis because almost all of the overdose data for this age group has been suppressed. Many states were also missing from the Fatal Drug Overdose Data, which only contained information from the years 2020-2024, during which the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted the collection of socioeconomic information. Thus, the reduced number of observations may have affected model stability.
Additionally, residual plots and Q-Q plots for the models (shown on the “Residual Plots” page) reveal there may have been some violations of the assumptions for linear regression, namely linearity of the data, homoscedasticity, and normality of the residuals. These violations do not appear to be catastrophic, but conclusions from the models, especially for heroin, prescription opioids, and benzodiazepines, should be interpreted with caution.

title: R Code
text:
Results

All drugs

# data cleaning 
data_hs = od_data %>%
  select(alldrug_rate, med_income, poverty, hs_grad) %>%
  drop_na()

#backward elimination
#regfit_hs = regsubsets(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + hs_grad, 
#                       data = data_hs, method = "backward")


# backward elimination using 10-fold cross validation
k = 10
set.seed(1)

folds = sample(1:k, nrow(data_hs), replace = TRUE)

cv.errors = matrix(NA, k, 3, dimnames = list(NULL, paste(1:3)))

# function for prediction with regsubsets
predict.regsubsets = function(object, newdata, id, ...){
  form = as.formula(object$call[[2]])
  mat = model.matrix(form, newdata)
  coefi = coef(object, id = id)
  xvars = names(coefi)
  mat[, xvars] %*% coefi
}

for(j in 1:k){
  best.fit = regsubsets(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + hs_grad, 
                        data = data_hs[folds != j, ],
                        nvmax = 3,
                        method = "backward")
  
  for(i in 1:3){
    pred = predict(best.fit, data_hs[folds == j, ], id = i)
    cv.errors[j, i] = mean((data_hs$alldrug_rate[folds == j] - pred)^2)
  }
}

mean.cv.errors = apply(cv.errors, 2, mean)

plot(mean.cv.errors, type = "b")

# perform best subset selection and obtain the model
reg.best_hs = regsubsets(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + hs_grad, 
                         data = data_hs, nvmax = 3, method = "backward")
coef(reg.best_hs, which.min(mean.cv.errors))


# obtained model
alldrug_model_hs = lm(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + hs_grad, 
                       data = data_hs)

summary(alldrug_model_hs)


# Test MSE, RMSE, MAE
cv.mae = rep(NA, k)

for(j in 1:k){
  fit = lm(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + hs_grad,
             data = data_hs[folds != j, ])
  
  pred = predict(fit, newdata = data_hs[folds == j, ])
  
  actual = data_hs$alldrug_rate[folds == j]
  
  cv.mae[j] = mean(abs(actual - pred))
}

alldrug_hs.mse = min(mean.cv.errors)
alldrug_hs.rmse = sqrt(alldrug_hs.mse)
alldrug_hs.mae = mean(cv.mae)
alldrug_hs.adjr2 = summary(alldrug_model_hs)$adj.r.squared

cat("Test MSE =", alldrug_hs.mse, "\n")
cat("Test RMSE =", alldrug_hs.rmse, "\n")
cat("Test MAE =", alldrug_hs.mae, "\n")
cat("Adjusted R-squared =", alldrug_hs.adjr2, "\n")


# data cleaning
data_bach = od_data %>%
  select(alldrug_rate, med_income, poverty, bachelors) %>%
  drop_na()

#backward elimination
#regfit_bach = regsubsets(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + bachelors, 
#                       data = data_bach, 
#                       method = "backward")

# backward elimination using 10-fold cross validation
k = 10
set.seed(1)

folds = sample(1:k, nrow(data_bach), replace = TRUE)

cv.errors = matrix(NA, k, 3, dimnames = list(NULL, paste(1:3)))

# function for prediction with regsubsets
predict.regsubsets = function(object, newdata, id, ...){
  form = as.formula(object$call[[2]])
  mat = model.matrix(form, newdata)
  coefi = coef(object, id = id)
  xvars = names(coefi)
  mat[, xvars] %*% coefi
}

for(j in 1:k){
  best.fit = regsubsets(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + bachelors, 
                        data = data_bach[folds != j, ],
                        nvmax = 3,
                        method = "backward")
  
  for(i in 1:3){
    pred = predict(best.fit, data_bach[folds == j, ], id = i)
    cv.errors[j, i] = mean((data_bach$alldrug_rate[folds == j] - pred)^2)
  }
}

mean.cv.errors = apply(cv.errors, 2, mean)

plot(mean.cv.errors, type = "b")

# perform best subset selection and obtain the model
reg.best = regsubsets(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + bachelors, 
                         data = data_bach, nvmax = 3, method = "backward")
coef(reg.best, which.min(mean.cv.errors))


# obtained model
alldrug_model_bach = lm(alldrug_rate ~ poverty + bachelors, 
                       data = data_bach)


summary(alldrug_model_bach)


# Test MSE, RMSE, MAE
cv.mae = rep(NA, k)

for(j in 1:k){
  fit = lm(alldrug_rate ~ poverty + bachelors,
           data = data_bach[folds != j, ])
  
  pred = predict(fit, newdata = data_bach[folds == j, ])
  
  actual = data_bach$alldrug_rate[folds == j]
  
  cv.mae[j] = mean(abs(actual - pred))
}

alldrug_bach.mse = min(mean.cv.errors)
alldrug_bach.rmse = sqrt(alldrug_bach.mse)
alldrug_bach.mae = mean(cv.mae)
alldrug_bach.adjr2 = summary(alldrug_model_bach)$adj.r.squared

cat("Test MSE =", alldrug_bach.mse, "\n")
cat("Test RMSE =", alldrug_bach.rmse, "\n")
cat("Test MAE =", alldrug_bach.mae, "\n")
cat("Adjusted R-squared =", alldrug_bach.adjr2, "\n")


# ridge hs

x = model.matrix(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + hs_grad,
                 data = data_hs)[, -1]

y = data_hs$alldrug_rate

set.seed(1)

# 10-fold cross-validation ridge regression
cv.out.ridge = cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, nfolds = 10)

# best lambda
bestlam.ridge = cv.out.ridge$lambda.min

# final ridge model
ridge.mod = glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, lambda = bestlam.ridge)

# coefficients
coef(ridge.mod)

# predictions using 10-fold CV-selected lambda
ridge.pred = predict(cv.out.ridge, s = "lambda.min", newx = x)

actual = y

# MSE, RMSE, MAE
alldrug_hs.ridge.mse = mean((actual - ridge.pred)^2)
alldrug_hs.ridge.rmse = sqrt(alldrug_hs.ridge.mse)
alldrug_hs.ridge.mae = mean(abs(actual - ridge.pred))

# R-squared
alldrug_hs.ridge.r2 = 1 - sum((actual - ridge.pred)^2) /
                sum((actual - mean(actual))^2)

# Adjusted R-squared
n = length(actual)
p = ncol(x)

alldrug_hs.ridge.adjr2 = 1 - ((1 - alldrug_hs.ridge.r2) * (n - 1)) /
                    (n - p - 1)


cat("MSE =", alldrug_hs.ridge.mse, "\n")
cat("RMSE =", alldrug_hs.ridge.rmse, "\n")
cat("MAE =", alldrug_hs.ridge.mae, "\n")
cat("Adjusted R-squared =", alldrug_hs.ridge.adjr2, "\n")


# ridge bachelors

x = model.matrix(alldrug_rate ~ med_income + poverty + bachelors,
                 data = data_bach)[, -1]

y = data_bach$alldrug_rate

set.seed(1)

# 10-fold cross-validation ridge regression
cv.out.ridge = cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, nfolds = 10)

# best lambda
bestlam.ridge = cv.out.ridge$lambda.min

# final ridge model
ridge.mod = glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, lambda = bestlam.ridge)

# coefficients
coef(ridge.mod)

# predictions using 10-fold CV-selected lambda
ridge.pred.alldrug = predict(cv.out.ridge, s = "lambda.min", newx = x)

actual.alldrug = y

# MSE, RMSE, MAE
alldrug_bach.ridge.mse = mean((actual.alldrug - ridge.pred.alldrug)^2)
alldrug_bach.ridge.rmse = sqrt(alldrug_bach.ridge.mse)
alldrug_bach.ridge.mae = mean(abs(actual.alldrug - ridge.pred.alldrug))

# R-squared
alldrug_bach.ridge.r2 = 1 - sum((actual.alldrug - ridge.pred.alldrug)^2) /
  sum((actual.alldrug - mean(actual.alldrug))^2)

# Adjusted R-squared
n = length(actual.alldrug)
p = ncol(x)

alldrug_bach.ridge.adjr2 = 1 - ((1 - alldrug_bach.ridge.r2) * (n - 1)) /
  (n - p - 1)


cat("MSE =", alldrug_bach.ridge.mse, "\n")
cat("RMSE =", alldrug_bach.ridge.rmse, "\n")
cat("MAE =", alldrug_bach.ridge.mae, "\n")
cat("Adjusted R-squared =", alldrug_bach.ridge.adjr2, "\n")


### stats comparisons

# baseline model
alldrug_baseline.pred = mean(od_data$alldrug_rate, na.rm = TRUE)

alldrug_baseline.pred.med = median(od_data$benzodiazepines_rate, na.rm = TRUE)


alldrug_baseline.rmse = sqrt(mean((od_data$alldrug_rate - alldrug_baseline.pred)^2,
                          na.rm = TRUE))

alldrug_baseline.mae = mean(abs(od_data$alldrug_rate - alldrug_baseline.pred.med),
                    na.rm = TRUE)

alldrug_baseline.adjr2 = 0


comparison_table = data.frame(
  Model = c("Baseline",
            "Linear regression with variable selection (hs_grad)",
            "Linear regression with variable selection (bachelors)",
            "Ridge regression (hs_grad)",
            "Ridge regression (bachelors)"),
  
  RMSE = c(alldrug_baseline.rmse,
           alldrug_hs.rmse,
           alldrug_bach.rmse,
           alldrug_hs.ridge.rmse,
           alldrug_bach.ridge.rmse),
  
  MAE = c(alldrug_baseline.mae,
          alldrug_hs.mae,
          alldrug_bach.mae,
          alldrug_hs.ridge.mae,
          alldrug_bach.ridge.mae),
  
  Adjusted_R2 = c(alldrug_baseline.adjr2,
                  alldrug_hs.adjr2,
                  alldrug_bach.adjr2,
                  alldrug_hs.ridge.adjr2,
                  alldrug_bach.ridge.adjr2)
)

knitr::kable(comparison_table,
             digits = 3,
             caption = '"All Drugs" Model Comparison')

text:
Results

age_long = od_data %>%
  select(age_15_24_rate,
         age_25_34_rate,
         age_35_44_rate,
         age_45_54_rate,
         age_55_64_rate,
         age_65plus_rate) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "age_group",
               values_to = "rate") %>%
  drop_na()

# Levene's test to see if variance is equal across groups
leveneTest(rate ~ age_group, data = age_long)

# assumption is violated, so do Welch's ANOVA
welch_age = oneway.test(rate ~ age_group,
                         data = age_long,
                         var.equal = FALSE)

welch_age

pairwise_age = pairwise.t.test(age_long$rate,
                                age_long$age_group,
                                p.adjust.method = "BH",
                                pool.sd = FALSE)

pairwise_age


pairwise_table = as.data.frame(as.table(pairwise_age$p.value))

pairwise_table = na.omit(pairwise_table)

colnames(pairwise_table) = c("Group_1", "Group_2", "Adjusted_p_value")

  age_15_24_rate = "Ages 15-24",
  age_25_34_rate = "Ages 25-34",

title: Relationships Between Socioeconomic and Demographic Variables and Drug Overdose Death Rates
text:
Over half of Americans report knowing someone personally who has died of an overdose (American Addiction Centers, 2024). Drug overdose deaths remain a major public health crisis in the United States, affecting individuals across all socioeconomic, demographic, and geographic backgrounds. Overdose mortality rates have risen steadily since at least 1999, with a dramatic upsurge seen in the past decade (see Figure 1). While this issue is often discussed in broad terms, frequently framed as a uniform epidemic affecting the population as a whole (e.g., the “opioid epidemic”), the underlying drivers of overdose risk are complex and multifaceted. In particular, socioeconomic factors such as income, poverty, and educational attainment may play an important role in shaping the prevalence and distribution of overdose deaths across the country. Understanding these relationships is critical for both informing policy and targeting interventions to protect vulnerable groups.
(National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2024)
This study aims to answer the following questions:

How are median household income, poverty rate, and educational attainment related to drug overdose death rates in states, and do these relationships vary across different drugs?
Does the effect of poverty on overdose death rates depend on educational attainment of the population?
Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between males and females?
Are there significant differences in overdose death rate between different age groups?
